event: Arab and Swahili sailors traversing the Indian Ocean become aware of the uninhabited Mascarene islands, which appear on early maps as Dina Arobi.
Portuguese navigators reach the island during their Indian Ocean expeditions and name it Cirne, though they establish no permanent settlement.
A Dutch fleet under Admiral Wybrand van Warwyck lands and names the island Mauritius after Prince Maurice of Nassau.
The Dutch establish their first permanent settlement, beginning systematic colonization and the exploitation of ebony forests.
The flightless dodo bird, endemic to the island, becomes extinct due to hunting and introduced animals, becoming a global symbol of human-caused extinction.
The Dutch abandon Mauritius after repeated hardships, leaving the island uninhabited again.
France claims the island and renames it Isle de France.
The French East India Company begins active settlement, importing enslaved Africans and Malagasy to develop plantations.
Governor Bertrand-Francois Mahe de La Bourdonnais develops Port Louis as a key naval base and harbour, spurring economic growth.
Control passes from the French East India Company to the French Crown, making Isle de France a royal colony.
British forces capture the island during the Napoleonic Wars after the naval Battle of Grand Port and a landing at Cap Malheureux.
The Treaty of Paris formally cedes the island to Britain, which restores its original name, Mauritius.
The arrival of the first indentured laborers from India begins a vast migration that reshapes the island's demographics.
Slavery is abolished, freeing roughly 70,000 enslaved people, with planters compensated by the British government.
A devastating malaria epidemic sweeps the island, killing tens of thousands and prompting public health reforms.
A new constitution introduces a partly elected Council of Government, expanding limited political representation.
Indian nationalist leader Mahatma Gandhi visits Mauritius, encouraging the Indo-Mauritian community to seek political rights.
Manilall Doctor arrives at Gandhi's urging to advocate for the rights of the Indian indentured population.
Eugene Laurent's Action Liberale movement campaigns for broader suffrage against the Franco-Mauritian oligarchy.
The Mauritius Labour Party is founded by Maurice Cure to defend the interests of workers and the underprivileged.
Riots erupt on sugar estates over wages and working conditions, leading to deaths and igniting the labor movement.
Strikes and unrest at the Belle Vue Harel sugar estate result in police killing several workers, galvanizing trade unionism.
A new constitution greatly expands the franchise, increasing the electorate and empowering the emerging majority communities.
Elections under the broadened franchise bring new representatives into the Legislative Council, weakening old elite dominance.
The first general election under universal adult suffrage is held, a landmark in Mauritian democratic development.
Britain detaches the Chagos Archipelago to form the British Indian Ocean Territory, later expelling its inhabitants.
Pro-independence parties led by Seewoosagur Ramgoolam win a decisive electoral victory over those favoring continued British association.
Mauritius becomes an independent nation within the Commonwealth, with Seewoosagur Ramgoolam as its first prime minister.
Mauritius is admitted to the United Nations as a sovereign member state.
Paul Berenger and others found the Mouvement Militant Mauricien, a leftist party that becomes a major political force.
A state of emergency is declared amid labor unrest, and MMM leaders are detained as the government cracks down on strikes.
Student protests over education and social issues spread across the island, signaling a new generation's activism.
An MMM-led coalition under Anerood Jugnauth sweeps the elections, ending the long dominance of the Labour Party.
Anerood Jugnauth forms the Militant Socialist Movement (MSM) after a split, reshaping the political landscape.
Mauritius becomes a republic within the Commonwealth, replacing the British monarch with a Mauritian president as head of state.
Riots erupt after the death in custody of popular Creole singer Kaya, exposing tensions over inequality among Afro-Mauritians.
An MSM-MMM alliance wins power, and Anerood Jugnauth returns as prime minister in a pre-agreed power-sharing deal with Paul Berenger.
Paul Berenger becomes prime minister, the first non-Hindu and first Franco-Mauritian to hold the office.
Navinchandra Ramgoolam, son of the founding prime minister, leads a Labour-led alliance to electoral victory.
Mauritius posts strong economic growth as it diversifies beyond sugar into textiles, tourism, and financial services.
Anerood Jugnauth's Lepep alliance wins the election, and he again becomes prime minister.
Pravind Jugnauth succeeds his father Anerood as prime minister, continuing the family's prominence in politics.
The International Court of Justice advises that Britain's continued administration of the Chagos Archipelago is unlawful.
The bulk carrier MV Wakashio runs aground and spills oil off the southeast coast, causing the island's worst environmental disaster.
Britain agrees to transfer sovereignty of the Chagos Archipelago to Mauritius, while retaining the Diego Garcia military base under lease.
An opposition alliance led by Navinchandra Ramgoolam wins a landslide, ousting Pravind Jugnauth's government.
